title: Heading outline parser (Core primitive)
description: Shared Core service that parses a markdown body into its heading structure: a flat, document-order list of headings each with level (1-6), title, the NSRange of the heading line, the NSRange of its section (heading line through the end of its subtree — everything up to the next heading of same-or-higher level), and its ancestor heading-path. Must be fence-aware (ignore '#' lines inside fenced code blocks). Pure and unit-tested; no AppKit. Unblocks refile (PUNK-7a2: needs section ranges + paths), heading folding (PUNK-czt: needs foldable ranges), and the inspector (PUNK-4bz: needs per-heading structure).
acceptance_criteria: Parses ATX headings with correct level/title/heading-range/section-range/path; section range spans the full subtree; '#' inside fenced code blocks is not treated as a heading; setext and edge cases handled or documented; unit-tested.
close_reason: HeadingOutline Core parser: flat document-order list of HeadingNode (level, title, headingRange, sectionRange spanning the full subtree, ancestor path), fence-aware (ignores # inside code fences), ATX-validated (requires space after #s, rejects >6 and indented). Pure/AppKit-free, 10 unit tests. Unblocks PUNK-7a2/czt/4bz. Tests: Core 146 green; lint clean.

title: Emacs motions: Meta word/sentence/paragraph navigation
description: Implement Emacs Meta-key motions on top of the dispatch layer: M-f/M-b (forward/backward word), M-d (kill word forward), M-DEL (backward-kill-word), M-a/M-e (sentence), M-{/M-} (paragraph), M-< / M-> (buffer start/end). macOS already provides the Control motions (C-a/C-e/C-f/C-b/C-n/C-p/C-k/C-d/C-t) natively, so this focuses on the Meta layer NSTextView doesn't ship. Word boundaries should match Emacs semantics reasonably.
acceptance_criteria: With Emacs mode on: M-f/M-b move by word, M-d/M-DEL kill word forward/back, M-a/M-e by sentence, M-</M-> jump to buffer ends; selections/marks update correctly.
close_reason: Meta motions implemented via pure Core EmacsMotion (caretDestination + killRange, 14 tests): M-f/M-b word, M-a/M-e sentence, M-{/M-} paragraph (blank-line delimited), M-</M-> buffer ends, M-d/M-DEL word kill (undoable delete; kill-ring integration lands in PUNK-2nn). Coordinator.performEmacsCommand applies them. Tests: Core 120 green; build + lint clean.

title: Emacs mark, region & kill-ring (C-space/C-w/M-w/C-y/M-y)
description: Implement the Emacs mark + kill-ring model: C-space sets the mark (activates region selection that extends with motion), C-w kills (cuts) the region, M-w copies the region, C-y yanks the most recent kill, M-y after a yank cycles the kill-ring (yank-pop), C-g cancels the mark/region. Maintain a kill-ring data structure; integrate with the system pasteboard for C-w/M-w/C-y so copy/paste interops with the OS. Word/line kills from the motions task should also push onto the kill-ring.
acceptance_criteria: With Emacs mode on: C-space sets mark and region grows with motion; C-w/M-w cut/copy region (and hit the system pasteboard); C-y yanks; M-y after C-y cycles prior kills; C-g cancels the region.
close_reason: Mark/region/kill-ring/yank implemented. Core: pure EmacsKillRing (newest-first, capacity eviction, yank-pop rotate; 7 tests). Coordinator: C-Space set-mark, motions extend region from mark, C-w kill / M-w copy region (→ kill-ring + system pasteboard), C-y yank, M-y yank-pop (tracked via lastYankRange + isPerformingEmacsEdit guard so user edits clear state but Emacs ops don't), C-g cancels mark/region, word kills (M-d/M-DEL) now feed the ring. Extracted Emacs dispatch into EditorCoordinator+Emacs.swift to keep the file/type within limits. Stateful AppKit wiring needs manual validation; kill-ring unit-tested. Tests: Core 127 green; build + lint clean (0 errors).

title: Emacs mode: persisted toggle + keybinding dispatch infrastructure
description: Foundation for Emacs mode. Add a persisted 'Emacs keybindings' setting (AppStorage/UserDefaults) and a menu toggle (until the Settings UI in PUNK-yf7 lands). Build a keybinding dispatch layer in the PillTextView/Coordinator that, when the mode is on, intercepts Control/Meta chords in keyDown/doCommand(by:) and routes them to Emacs command handlers; when off, behavior is unchanged. Must not disrupt IME/marked-text composition. This task wires the plumbing + the toggle; specific commands land in the sibling tasks.
acceptance_criteria: A persisted Emacs-mode toggle (menu item) exists; with it on, a dispatch layer receives C-/M- chords in the editor and can consume or pass them through; with it off, no behavior change; marked-text/IME composition unaffected.
close_reason: Emacs dispatch infrastructure + toggle. Core: EmacsCommand vocabulary, EmacsKeyChord, pure EmacsKeymap (dictionary lookup, 6 tests). App: NSEvent→chord decoder, PillTextView.keyDown interception (skips IME + active completion popover), Coordinator.performEmacsCommand dispatch (implements keyboardQuit/C-g; recognized-but-unimplemented commands consumed so Meta chords don't insert garbage), @AppStorage('editor.emacsKeybindings') persisted toggle + Editor menu item, threaded through the representable. Extracted PillTextView to its own file. Tests: Core 106 green; build clean; lint clean.

title: Preview: clickable links — external URLs, [[wikilinks]], #tags
description: Make links work in the MarkdownUI preview. Standard [text](url) should open external URLs in the browser. Preprocess the rendered body to convert [[wikilinks]] and #tags into markdown links with a custom scheme (punk://note/<title>, punk://tag/<name>), then handle them via an OpenURLAction: note links select the note (or offer create like the editor's click-to-create), tag links set the sidebar filter to tag:<name>, everything else opens externally. Reuse WikilinkDecorator regexes / AppState.createNote / sidebarFilterQuery.
acceptance_criteria: In preview: external links open in the default browser; [[wikilinks]] navigate to the note (or prompt to create when missing); #tags set the sidebar filter; alias links [[Target|alias]] resolve to Target.
close_reason: Preview links now work. New Core PreviewLinkRewriter converts [[wikilinks]] and #tags to punk://note/ and punk://tag/ markdown links, skipping fenced code blocks and inline code spans (so #include/[[x]] in samples aren't mangled). MarkdownPreviewView routes punk:// via OpenURLAction (note→open/create, tag→sidebar filter) and lets everything else open externally. Tests: Core 100 green (+9 rewriter).

title: Preview: tree-sitter syntax highlighting in fenced code blocks
description: Implement a MarkdownUI CodeSyntaxHighlighter that statically highlights each code block. MarkdownUI hands us (code, language); resolve the language via the shared TreeSitterMarkdownHighlighter.makeLanguageProvider(), parse the code with SwiftTreeSitter (Parser->Tree), run the config's .highlights Query, collect captures (range+name), and build an AttributedString applying the same color mapping (attributes(for:theme:)) the editor uses. Falls back to plain monospaced text for unknown/unsupported languages. No markdown injection needed (MarkdownUI already splits code blocks).
acceptance_criteria: Code fences in the preview pane colorize for every editor-supported language (swift/python/ts/js/rust/c/cpp/bash/json); unknown languages render as plain mono text; colors match the editor's Dracula palette.
close_reason: MarkdownUI CodeSyntaxHighlighter (TreeSitterCodeSyntaxHighlighter) backed by a new static span extractor TreeSitterMarkdownHighlighter.codeHighlightSpans (parse + highlights query + predicate resolution). Wired into MarkdownPreviewView; shares the editor's grammar/Dracula palette. Pure static helpers marked nonisolated so the preview path runs off the live @MainActor highlighter. Tests: Infra 115 green (+4 static-highlight).

title: Epic: Editor navigation + full Emacs keybinding mode
description: Add a toggleable, full Emacs keybinding mode to the NSTextView-based editor (user is an Emacs user). macOS NSTextView already provides C-a/C-e/C-f/C-b/C-n/C-p/C-k/C-d/C-t and basic C-y; Emacs mode layers on the Meta motions, an explicit mark + kill-ring, yank/yank-pop, and a handful of editing commands. Gated behind a persisted setting so non-Emacs users are unaffected.
acceptance_criteria: A persisted 'Emacs mode' toggle exists; when on, Meta word-motions, set-mark/region, kill-ring (C-w/M-w/C-y/M-y), and core editing chords work in the editor; when off, default macOS behavior is unchanged; IME/marked-text composition is not disrupted.
close_reason: All children complete: PUNK-9y5 (toggle + dispatch infra), PUNK-0cy (Meta motions), PUNK-2nn (mark/region/kill-ring/yank), PUNK-9ls (undo/save/case/transpose). Full Emacs keybinding mode behind a persisted toggle; key→command mapping and all text math are pure/unit-tested in Core, AppKit wiring is a thin shell. Stateful interaction (mark/region/yank, C-x prefix) needs manual in-app validation.

title: Epic: Preview rendering polish (syntax highlighting, working links, theme)
description: The read-only MarkdownUI preview pane (MarkdownPreviewView, also used for chat messages) currently renders with the stock .gitHub theme: code blocks are unstyled, links are not wired to navigation, and the palette doesn't match the editor. Bring preview to parity with the editor: tree-sitter code highlighting, clickable links (external + internal wikilinks/tags), and a theme consistent with the editor (Dracula/dark-mode aware). Reuses the language provider + color mapping from TreeSitterMarkdownHighlighter.
acceptance_criteria: Code fences in preview are syntax-highlighted for all editor-supported languages; standard [text](url) links open externally; [[wikilinks]] navigate (or offer create) and #tags filter the sidebar; preview palette matches the editor theme in light and dark mode.
close_reason: All children complete: PUNK-3wk (code syntax highlighting), PUNK-2a5 (clickable links), PUNK-ku1 (theme alignment). Preview now matches the editor: tree-sitter code highlighting, working wikilink/tag/external links, and an EditorTheme-derived MarkdownUI theme.

title: Editor body text illegible in dark mode
description: Reported with screenshot: in dark mode, all body text (paragraphs, list items, status lines) rendered dim/illegible while only headings stayed bright. Root cause: Neon's TextStorageSystemInterface resets each styled range to textView.typingAttributes before applying token colors; the editor never set an explicit adaptive foreground in typingAttributes, so untokenized body text inherited a fallback that's illegible on a dark background. Heading text was fine only because tree-sitter applies an explicit .textColor to the text.title token.
close_reason: Fixed: editor now sets explicit typingAttributes ({mono font, .foregroundColor: .textColor}) and seeds content via setAttributedString with the same base attrs, so Neon's reset-to-typingAttributes uses an adaptive foreground. Added HybridUXDecorator.fillBodyColor as a safety net that gives any range left without a foreground an adaptive .textColor (without overriding tree-sitter token colors). 2 new regression tests; all 91 Infra tests pass.

title: Editor W4: Wikilink pill chips + tag chips + in-editor autocomplete
description:
PKM-specific syntax. The hardest piece of the build — budget the full week.

Wikilinks:
- Render `[[Note]]` and `[[Note|alias]]` as a pill chip via NSTextAttachment with a custom NSTextAttachmentCellProtocol cell. Cell draws a rounded background, the link text, and (if alias present) shows the alias.
- Caret-inside-range: collapse the chip and reveal raw `[[ ]]` source. Caret-outside-range: show chip, hide brackets via zero-width kerning or attachment replacement.
- Click on chip opens the target note (calls into the existing document repository).
- Unresolved links (target file does not exist) render with a distinct style (e.g., dashed underline) and click-to-create.

Autocomplete:
- `[[` triggers a fuzzy-match popover anchored to the caret. Source: existing FTS5 title index (or trigram index — whichever the SQLiteSearchIndex actor supplies).
- ↑/↓/Enter to select; Esc dismisses.
- Continue to update results as the user types after `[[`.

Tags:
- `#tag` and `#nested/tag` render as a lower-contrast colored pill (color derived from hash for stability).
- Autocomplete on `#` once the third character is typed; backed by a tags table maintained by the background indexer.

This sub-issue SUPERSEDES the in-editor autocomplete half of PUNK-913. The backlinks panel + link graph table portion of PUNK-913 remains as a separate side-panel feature.
acceptance_criteria:
- [[Existing Note]] renders as a pill chip when caret is outside; reverts to `[[Existing Note]]` source when caret enters
- Click on chip opens the target note in the editor
- [[Nonexistent]] renders with the unresolved style; click prompts to create
- Typing `[[fo` shows an autocomplete popover with fuzzy matches; Enter inserts `[[Foo Note]]`
- #tag renders as a colored pill; click filters notes by tag (or opens a tag view, deferred to a separate issue)
- Autocomplete latency < 50ms p99 on a 10k-note vault
close_reason: Visual + click-to-open core delivered (per scoping decision): WikilinkPillLayoutManager draws rounded pill backgrounds (TextKit 1) for [[wikilinks]] and #tags; WikilinkDecorator applies pills when caret is outside and reveals raw source when caret enters; resolved links use accent color, unresolved get a dashed underline; #tags get stable hash-derived colors. Click on a resolved pill opens the target note (PillTextView hit-testing). Editor rebuilt on an explicit TK1 stack to host the custom layout manager. 15 new tests pass. Deferred to follow-ups: [[ autocomplete (PUNK-06f), click-to-create for unresolved (PUNK-1wc), tag autocomplete + click-to-filter (PUNK-y4d).

title: Editor W5: ⌘F find/replace (NSTextFinder) + ⌘O quick-open
description:
Editor-level search affordances. Uses TextKit 1's built-in NSTextFinder for free.

Find/Replace (⌘F, ⌘⌥F):
- Wire NSTextFinder against the editor's NSTextView. Free find bar, regex toggle, case-insensitive option, find-all-highlight.
- CRITICAL: search must operate over `textStorage.string` (raw source), NOT the displayed/decorated runs. This fixes the "Bear bug" where search misses content inside dimmed/hidden markers (e.g., searching `foo` in `**foo**` when the `**` is hidden).
- Replace operates on the underlying string; decorations recompute via the usual textStorage delegate path.

Quick Open (⌘O):
- Modal sheet, single text field, fuzzy match against vault titles via the existing SQLiteSearchIndex (FTS5).
- Looks/feels like Xcode's "Open Quickly".
- Enter opens the file in the active editor pane.

Explicitly out of scope: migrating SQLiteSearchIndex from raw SQLite to GRDB.swift (the research doc suggests this — defer; it's a separate decision the codebase already has a working answer for).
acceptance_criteria:
- ⌘F opens the find bar; finds matches inside dimmed/hidden markdown markers
- Regex search works via NSTextFinder's built-in toggle
- ⌘⌥F opens replace UI; replacements update the textStorage and trigger normal re-decoration
- ⌘O opens fuzzy quick-open over vault titles; ↑/↓/Enter navigation
- All flows respect VoiceOver
close_reason: Delivered: (1) ⌘O Quick Open palette wired through new QuickOpenView + QuickOpenMatcher (Core) — fuzzy subsequence match with word-boundary and consecutive-run bonuses, ↑/↓/Enter navigation, Esc dismisses. (2) ⌘F find/replace handled by NSTextView's standard NSTextFinder (already had usesFindBar=true + isIncrementalSearchingEnabled=true since W1). Moved 'Find by Content' from ⌘⌥F to ⌘⌥⇧F so ⌘⌥F is free for the standard Edit > Find and Replace menu. 10 matcher tests + UI test pass. Search operates on textStorage.string so dimmed markers are still searchable (fixes the 'Bear bug').

title: Editor W2: Parser pipeline — swift-markdown + Neon + tree-sitter-markdown
description:
Replace W1's regex stub with the real parsing pipeline.

Two parsers, two purposes:
1. swift-markdown (Apple, cmark-gfm under the hood) — canonical AST. Used on save/open/export. Correctness path; ~1ms parse for a 50KB note is fine.
2. Neon + SwiftTreeSitter + tree-sitter-markdown — live in-editor styling. Incremental, low-latency. Tree-sitter is fast+approximate for Markdown; pair with swift-markdown for canonical decisions.

Bridge implementation:
- On `textStorage(_:didProcessEditing:range:changeInLength:)`, feed the edit delta to `TreeSitterClient.didChangeContent(in:delta:)`
- Receive an invalidation RangeSet; reapply attributes only to those ranges (do NOT rehighlight the whole document)
- Token → attribute table: heading, emphasis, strong, code_span, fenced_code, link, image, list_item, etc. all map to NSAttributedString.Key entries
- Pass plain `String` + `[HighlightRange]` across actor boundaries — never NSAttributedString (not Sendable)

Libraries: github.com/swiftlang/swift-markdown, github.com/ChimeHQ/Neon, github.com/tree-sitter/tree-sitter-markdown grammar.
acceptance_criteria:
- Incremental highlight latency p99 < 50ms on the edited paragraph of a 100KB note
- swift-markdown canonical parse runs on save (and optionally on open) and feeds future export/Quick Look paths
- Highlight survives all CommonMark + GFM constructs in a fixture corpus (headings, lists, tables, links, images, fences, blockquotes, hr)
- W1's regex pipeline is removed; no double-styling races
close_reason: Delivered: swift-markdown + Neon + tree-sitter-markdown wired in. New TreeSitterMarkdownHighlighter (Infra) wraps Neon.TextViewHighlighter for incremental live styling. New CanonicalMarkdownParser (Core) runs on save via swift-markdown. RegexSyntaxHighlighter + protocol removed. 20 new tests pass (7 canonical-parser + 13 highlighter). Editor preview UI test passes. Wikilink/tag styling intentionally deferred to PUNK-6du (W4).

title: Editor W3: Hybrid UX — marker dimming, heading sizes, code blocks, link styling
description:
The "Bear / iA Writer feel" layer. Markers stay visible but visually recede; caret proximity reveals them at full contrast.

Deliverables:
- Caret-proximity marker dimming: for each token whose range is more than ~2 chars from the caret, set foregroundColor to NSColor.tertiaryLabelColor; within proximity, NSColor.labelColor. Recompute on every selection change.
- Heading font scaling: H1 ~1.7x, H2 ~1.4x, H3 ~1.2x, H4–H6 baseline + bold. Same family as body for visual coherence.
- Bold/italic glyph weights: synthesize via NSFontManager when the user's editor font lacks a true italic/bold cut
- Fenced code blocks: monospaced font + subtle background fill (paragraph style with .backgroundColor). Inline token coloring via Splash or Highlightr keyed on the fence's language tag
- Inline code: monospaced + background tint
- Links: underline + accent color; blockquote: left border + indent + dimmer foreground; horizontal rules: full-width thin line via paragraph spacing

Decoration model: keep a `RangeSet` of decoration ranges and recompute diffs on selection change. Reapply attributes only to the diff (mirrors CodeMirror 6's Decoration model in pattern).
acceptance_criteria:
- Markers visibly dim when caret moves away, reveal when caret approaches (≤2 chars)
- Headings render at appropriate scaled sizes
- Fenced ```swift / ```python / ```js blocks have syntax-colored content
- Code/quote/HR all visually distinct from body text
- Decoration recompute on selection change completes within 16ms (one frame)
close_reason: Delivered: HybridUXDecorator (Infra) overlays per-heading font scaling, caret-proximity marker dimming (≤2 chars reveals), blockquote color, and horizontal-rule color on top of TreeSitterMarkdownHighlighter. Wired through RawEditorView.Coordinator on text/selection changes. Code-block backgrounds shipped in W2. 12 new tests pass — perf test confirms decoration on 500-line doc completes in well under one frame (16ms budget). Code-fence CONTENT syntax coloring (the per-language injection piece) carved out into PUNK-tlb so the decorator could ship clean.

title: Editor W1: NSTextView wrapper + basic shell (load/save, regex highlight, frontmatter dim)
description:
Foundation week. Goal: a working editable .md surface inside SwiftUI with stub styling.

Deliverables:
- `MarkdownTextView: NSViewRepresentable` wrapping `NSTextView.scrollableTextView()`
- `NSTextView.isRichText = false` — we own all attributes
- NSTextStorage delegate hook (`textStorage(_:didProcessEditing:range:changeInLength:)`)
- File I/O: load a `.md` from disk into an `NSAttributedString`, save back to disk preserving line endings + trailing newline; coordinator handles change tracking and dirty state
- Stub regex-based highlighting for `#` headings (font size by `#` count), `**bold**`, `*italic*`, `` `code` `` — debounced 50ms, full document; this is the fallback path and is replaced by Neon in W2
- Frontmatter detection: YAML block between `---` lines rendered with dimmed foreground color + monospaced font
- Swift 6 strict concurrency: NSTextView and its delegate are @MainActor; future parsing actor lives off-main

Explicitly out of scope: tree-sitter, swift-markdown integration, marker dimming based on caret proximity, pill chips, autocomplete.
acceptance_criteria:
- Open + edit + save round-trips a .md file with no content loss (whitespace, CRLF, trailing newline preserved)
- Headings, bold, italic, inline code visibly highlighted within 100ms of opening a 50KB note
- Frontmatter block dimmed when present
- Undo (⌘Z) and redo (⌘⇧Z) work via NSTextView's built-in UndoManager
- VoiceOver reads document content correctly
close_reason: Already implemented. Sources/PunkRecordsApp/Views/RawEditorView.swift:154 defines EditorTextViewRepresentable: NSViewRepresentable wrapping NSTextView.scrollableTextView() with a Coordinator on NSTextViewDelegate. Packages/PunkRecordsInfra/Sources/PunkRecordsInfra/Editor/RegexSyntaxHighlighter.swift provides the regex-based highlighter. Load/save round-trip and dirty-state tracking happen via DocumentEditorViewModel. The editor epic effectively starts at PUNK-qpz (W2 — parser pipeline with swift-markdown + Neon).

title: Epic: Markdown editor — NSTextView + TextKit 1 + swift-markdown + Neon (Option B)
description:
Build the core editor surface for PunkRecords using TextKit 1, wrapped in SwiftUI via NSViewRepresentable. Hybrid-markdown UX (markers visible, dimmed near caret; wikilinks as pill chips when caret is outside; live-typed styling) modeled on Bear (pre-Panda) / iA Writer / Drafts.

Key decisions (see PunkRecords_Wysiwyg_research.md):
- TextKit 1 over TextKit 2. Evidence: Krzyżanowski's 4-year retrospective (7 unresolved FB radars); AltStore's public regret; MarkEdit explicitly rejecting TK2 for CodeMirror; Apple's own TextEdit falls back to TK1 for tables/printing per WWDC22 #10090.
- swift-markdown (cmark-gfm) for canonical parse on save/open/export — correctness path.
- Neon + SwiftTreeSitter + tree-sitter-markdown for live in-editor styling — incremental, low-latency path.
- MarkdownUI used ONLY for read-only preview panes (chat assistant messages, Quick Look), never the editor.
- NOT pursuing Option A (SwiftUI TextEditor) — pre-macOS 26 it cannot do pill chips / replaced ranges; revisit for v2.
- NOT pursuing Option C (WKWebView + CodeMirror 6) — accepts non-native feel + permanent JS/Swift maintenance split. CM6 is the strongest fallback only if Option B blows up.
- NOT pursuing Option D (native block editor) — wrong cost class for solo dev, wrong model for flat-file vault.

Unblocks: PUNK-czt (folding), PUNK-4bz (Inspector ⌘I integration), PUNK-ubz (DataDetectors), Week 4 of this epic supersedes the autocomplete half of PUNK-913 (backlinks panel remains in PUNK-913).

Fallback plan (documented in research §7) if Weeks 1–2 slip: regex-only highlighting on NSTextView, swift-markdown only at save time, no marker hiding, no pill chips — ~1500–2500 LOC, ships in 2 weeks. Tracked here as a contingency, not a separate issue.
acceptance_criteria:
- A vault .md file can be opened, edited, and saved through the new editor
- Hybrid styling: bold/italic/headings render in-place with markers visible but dimmed when caret is far
- [[wikilinks]] appear as pill chips when caret is outside; revert to source when caret enters
- ⌘F find/replace operates over raw source (catches matches inside dimmed markers — fixes the "Bear bug")
- 10MB single file remains responsive (incremental highlighting p99 < 50ms on edited paragraph)
- VoiceOver navigation works on headings and links (NSTextView accessibility free-tier)

title: Image attachment handler (HEIC normalize, resize, provider routing)
description:
Handler for user-attached images.

Accepted formats: PNG, JPEG, GIF, WebP, HEIC. HEIC normalized to JPEG before sending (broadest provider compatibility).

Resize policy:
- Anthropic: max edge 1568px per [vision docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/vision); resize larger images preserving aspect ratio; never upscale
- OpenAI: provider auto-handles, but resize anything > 2048px max-edge to save tokens
- Apple Foundation Models: no image input — surface "this provider doesn't support images; switch to Claude or GPT?" with a switch-provider button

Chip metadata: thumbnail (40px), dimensions (e.g. "1024 × 768"), file size in KB/MB.

Provider payload: image content block with base64 data + media type. Capability flag on LLMProvider: `nativeImageInput: Bool`.

Multi-image: each image is its own attachment chip; ordering preserved in the request.

Out of scope for v1: animated GIF handling (send first frame), image OCR pre-pass, screenshot annotation.
acceptance_criteria:
- PNG, JPEG, HEIC all attach with thumbnail + dimensions in chip
- HEIC converted to JPEG before send (verify base64 payload media type)
- Image >1568px max-edge resized for Anthropic provider
- Switching to Apple Foundation Models with images in composer surfaces blocking dialog with switch-provider CTA
- Multi-image conversation sends ordered image blocks in request

title: PDF attachment handler (provider-native + PDFKit fallback)
description:
Handler that routes a user-attached PDF to the right path based on provider capability.

Provider routing:
- Anthropic: send as `document` content block per [their PDF support](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/pdf-support) — up to 32MB and 100 pages
- OpenAI Responses API: send as `input_file` with file_data field
- Apple Foundation Models (text-only): extract text via PDFKit (`PDFDocument(url:).string`) and send as text block
- Capability detection: introduce `LLMCapabilities.nativePDFInput: Bool` on each provider; orchestrator picks path

Metadata for the chip:
- Page count (PDFKit can give this fast)
- File size
- Encrypted/locked detection — prompt for password, or refuse with clear message if blank-password unlock fails

Size policy:
- Hard cap at 32MB (Anthropic limit; OpenAI is generous; PDFKit fine with anything)
- Warn over 100 pages

Extracted-text path: preserve heading order by walking PDFKit's page-by-page text; do not attempt OCR for scanned PDFs in v1 — surface "this PDF appears to be scanned (no text extractable); image-OCR is not yet supported."
acceptance_criteria:
- Anthropic provider: PDF goes through as document content block (verifiable in wire trace)
- OpenAI provider: PDF goes through as input_file (verifiable in wire trace)
- Apple Foundation Models: PDF text-extracted; chip shows "(text extracted, N pages)"
- 32MB / 100-page warnings fire at correct thresholds
- Encrypted PDF surfaces a password prompt; cancelling cancels the attachment
- Scanned (no-text) PDF surfaces an explicit "no text extractable" error rather than sending empty content

title: Chat composer attachment UX (chips, drag-drop, file picker, ⌘⇧A)
description:
SwiftUI composer extension for the chat surface.

Functionality:
- Drop zone: `.onDrop(of: [.fileURL, .image, .pdf])` on the composer container; visible overlay when a drag is in progress
- File picker: ⌘⇧A and a toolbar paperclip button open NSOpenPanel via `.fileImporter`; allowedContentTypes derived from registered handlers; allowsMultipleSelection true
- Attachment chips: HStack above the text field; each chip shows SF Symbol for type, filename (truncating middle), byte size, remove ✕
- Token estimate: live label in the composer footer ("~12.4k tokens"); updates as attachments and text change; uses existing TokenEstimator plus provider-specific image/PDF cost adapters
- Confirmation: if total estimated tokens > 50k OR any single attachment > 20MB, show NSAlert before send
- Persistence: each accepted attachment generates a security-scoped bookmark stored alongside the message in the chat transcript file (`<!-- attachment: {bookmark-base64, filename, size, type} -->` adjacent to the message text)

Does NOT do file content reading or provider payload building — those live in the per-type handler sub-issues. This issue is purely the input surface + state management.
acceptance_criteria:
- Dragging a file over the composer shows a visible drop affordance
- Dropping one or more files adds chips with correct icon, filename, and size
- ⌘⇧A opens NSOpenPanel scoped to registered content types
- Removing a chip removes the attachment from the pending message state
- Token estimate updates within 100ms of attachment change
- Confirmation NSAlert appears for >50k tokens or >20MB single file
- Security-scoped bookmark stored with the message; resolvable on reopen

title: Text/code/markdown attachment handler
description:
Handler that converts a user-attached text/code/markdown file into a text block on the outgoing LLM request.

Responsibilities:
- Accept UTF-8 file contents from a security-scoped URL
- Detect binary content (NULL bytes in the first 8KB, or failed UTF-8 decode) and reject with a clear error
- Wrap content in a fenced block with a heading: filename + relative path from user's home dir (or absolute if outside ~)
- Surface byte count and estimated tokens back to the composer for the chip display
- File size policy: warn at 1MB, hard-cap at 10MB (configurable)

File types in v1: any UTF-8 text — .md, .txt, .swift, .py, .js, .ts, .json, .yaml, .toml, .html, .css, .sh, .rb, .go, .rs, .c, .cpp, .h, .java, .kt, .sql, .csv, .log, .xml. Driven by UTI conformance to `public.text` plus a small allowlist of source-code UTIs that don't inherit from public.text.

Not in scope here: PDF, image, .docx/.pages/.rtf. Those are separate handlers.
acceptance_criteria:
- Plain .txt + a Swift source file + a YAML config all attach as text blocks with correct filename headings
- Binary detection rejects a .o or .png attempted as text with a clear error message
- 1MB file warns; 11MB file is hard-rejected with explanation
- Non-UTF-8 (latin1) file rejected with "encoding not supported" rather than mojibake
- Estimated tokens reported back to composer match TokenEstimator(content)

title: Epic: File attachments in chat composer (read-only)
description:
Let users attach external files to a chat turn via drag-drop into the composer or NSOpenPanel ("Attach…" button / ⌘⇧A). Attachments are strictly read-only — PunkRecords never writes to the source location. The vault remains the only write surface.

Three attachment types, each provider-aware:
- Text / markdown / code → load as UTF-8 string, send as text block with filename + relative-path heading
- PDF → if provider advertises native PDF input (Anthropic, OpenAI), send as document block; otherwise extract text via PDFKit and send as text
- Image (PNG, JPEG, GIF, WebP, HEIC) → resize to provider limits, normalize HEIC→JPEG, send as image block; on text-only providers (Apple Foundation Models today), surface an explicit "this provider doesn't support images — switch?" instead of silently dropping.

Composer UX:
- Drag overlay appears when files are dragged over the composer (accepts .fileURL, .image, .pdf)
- Multi-file attach in one drop / one picker invocation
- Each attachment shows as a removable chip above the text input (filename, type icon, byte size, ✕)
- Live token-estimate updates as attachments add/remove
- Confirmation prompt before send if estimated tokens > ~50k

Persistence:
- Attachments travel with the chat message in its on-disk transcript
- Reference by absolute path + security-scoped bookmark (so re-opens survive macOS sandbox reset and minor user moves)
- If a source file is missing on re-render, show "[file unavailable]" placeholder; do not auto-snapshot bytes into the transcript by default
- Future opt-in: "snapshot" mode that copies into `.punkrecords/attachments/{conversation-id}/` for archival

Provider integration notes:
- LLMOrchestrator + each LLMProvider gains a Capabilities surface declaring nativeImageInput / nativePDFInput / maxAttachmentBytes
- LLMRequest message blocks expand from text-only to (text | image | document)
- Existing AgentTool stack is unaffected — agent self-driven reads still go through ReadDocumentTool; user-driven attachments are the new path

Why: the chat surface currently sees only vault content via tools. Users want to bring outside material in — "read this paper", "look at this screenshot", "summarize this transcript" — without first copying it into the vault. Read-only attachment respects the user's filesystem while letting the agent reach anywhere.

Explicit scope cuts:
- No audio/video transcription (separate feature; Granola-style)
- No .docx/.pages/.rtf/.epub conversion (users can paste content)
- No server-side caching of attachment bytes — each turn re-reads from disk
- No write-back to source files (the user's emphasis; verifiable invariant)

Tracked via sub-issues for composer UX, text handler, PDF handler, image handler.
acceptance_criteria:
- Drag a mix of .md + .pdf + .png onto the composer → three chips appear → token estimate updates → send → LLM responds with awareness of all three
- ⌘⇧A and "Attach…" menu item open NSOpenPanel with relevant content types
- Files NOT written outside the vault under any circumstance — verified by filesystem audit test (no FileHandle for writing opened against any path outside the vault root during attachment flow)
- Reopening a chat with attachments resolves files via security-scoped bookmark; missing files show a clear "[file unavailable]" placeholder
- Switching provider mid-conversation surfaces a clear error if an existing attachment type is unsupported by the new provider

title: Refile picker (⌘⇧M) — fuzzy move heading to (file, heading-path)
description:
Modal sheet, single text field. Caret on a heading + ⌘⇧M → fuzzy-matched results showing `File ▸ Heading ▸ Sub-heading` paths. Enter moves the heading (and its sub-tree) to the selected location.

Looks and feels like Xcode's "Open Quickly" (⌘⇧O). Single NSTextField + filtered list view.

Why: per PunkRecords_Orgmode_Lessons_Translation.md, this is the Mac-native form of org-refile. Apple Notes' "Move Note…", Things' "Move to project…", OmniFocus reorganize-by-keyboard all use this pattern. Drag-and-drop in the sidebar handles whole-file moves; refile-by-picker is for sub-heading granularity where dragging is awkward.

Trick to get right: when the moved heading is the target of `[[wikilinks]]` from other notes, either update the links transparently or surface the would-be breakage and ask. Don't silently break links.
acceptance_criteria:
- ⌘⇧M opens the picker only when the caret is on a heading
- Fuzzy match across all (file, heading-path) tuples in the vault
- Move writes both source and destination atomically
- Wikilinks targeting the moved heading either auto-update or surface a confirmation dialog
- Undo restores the heading to its original location and rolls back any link updates

title: Capture templates (filterable picker on quick-capture)
description:
Templates are markdown files in `.punkrecords/templates/` with frontmatter declaring fields (title, body shape, destination directory, optional summarize_url binding). Ship with defaults: Note, Journal entry, Meeting note, Web summary (URL field), Task, Idea.

Quick-capture composer (PUNK-5pr) becomes a two-stage UI: first keystrokes filter the template list; selecting a template (or enter on empty input → default "Note") opens its structured form.

Users author new templates by writing markdown files in the templates directory — no DSL, no special editor.

Why: per PunkRecords_Orgmode_Lessons_Translation.md, this is the Mac-native form of org-capture. Pattern is familiar from Raycast / Alfred / Drafts / Things Quick Entry. The Web summary template is the bridge between the capture surface and the URL→summary epic (PUNK-6rh).
acceptance_criteria:
- Quick-capture panel shows template list filtered by typed text
- Selecting a template opens its form; submit writes the file to the template's destination
- Templates are plain markdown files; user can author/edit them from the editor
- "Web summary" template triggers summarize_url (PUNK-ddq) and opens the resulting note
- Default ship-list: Note, Journal entry, Meeting note, Web summary, Task, Idea

title: Heading folding in editor (outline disclosure)
description:
Editor supports heading folding via disclosure indicators. ⌘. toggles the heading under the caret; ⌘⌥. folds all. Folded ranges visually collapse but remain searchable (find/replace + FTS still hit folded content).

Why: table-stakes for any markdown editor with structured documents. SwiftUI OutlineGroup or NSTextLayoutManager range-hiding makes this nearly free. Bear, Craft, iA Writer, Pages, Apple Notes all ship this — per the translation guide, "just don't skip it."

Implementation is editor-type-specific so this depends on the upcoming markdown-editor decision (TextKit 2 vs WKWebView+CodeMirror etc.).
acceptance_criteria:
- Disclosure chevron beside each heading
- ⌘. toggles the current heading
- ⌘⌥. folds all headings in the document
- Folded text is excluded from visual range but still hit by ⌘F find and by FTS
- Fold state is per-document and persists across editor reopen (session, not on-disk)

title: Sparse-tree sidebar search (Xcode Project Navigator pattern)
description:
Vault-wide search field at the top of the sidebar. As the user types, the outline filters: matching headings stay visible, ancestors stay visible to preserve context, everything else collapses. Parent nodes show hit-count badges (Mail unread-counts style).

⌘⇧F focuses the sidebar field. ⌘F still does in-document find. Click a result → editor opens the file scrolled and highlighted at the match.

Why: per PunkRecords_Orgmode_Lessons_Translation.md, this is the Mac-native form of Org-mode's sparse-tree search — a pattern Mac users already know from Xcode Project Navigator, Finder column filter, Mail filtered threads. Hierarchical context (file → H1 → H2 → matched H3) is the whole point; flat result lists lose the meaning of the match.

Implementation: SwiftUI OutlineGroup driven by a filtered data source backed by the existing FTS5 index for relevance ranking.
acceptance_criteria:
- ⌘⇧F focuses sidebar search
- Filter live (debounced ≤50ms) on a 10k-note vault
- Ancestors of matches stay visible; non-matching siblings collapse
- Hit-count badges on parent folders
- Click a result jumps to the file scrolled to the matched line, with the match highlighted
close_reason: Shipped in 2a7d4d9: SidebarFilter pure-function module in Core (11 passing unit tests covering case/diacritic insensitivity, hierarchical context, hit-count correctness, 10k-doc perf floor at 22ms). VaultBrowserView gained a search field with Cmd+Shift+F focus, auto-expanded DisclosureGroups during filter, hit-count badges. Modal SearchView (FTS5 body search) relocated to Cmd+Option+F as 'Find by Content…' so the two search surfaces have distinct keybindings.

title: Smart Notes — saved searches via NSPredicateEditor
description:
Sidebar section listing saved structured queries. "New Smart Note…" opens a sheet using NSPredicateEditor (the Finder / Mail / Photos rule-builder). Predicates compose over: tag, status, frontmatter key, date created/modified, scheduled, due, full-text match.

Ship with built-in smart notes: Inbox, Today, This Week, Untagged, Recently Captured, Web Summaries.

Persistence: each smart note is a markdown file in `Smart Notes/{name}.md` with frontmatter holding the predicate as a serialized structure. Plain-text on disk, Mac-native rule-builder UI on top.

Why: per PunkRecords_Orgmode_Lessons_Translation.md, no PKM in the competitive set surfaces the macOS-native rule-builder for saved searches — they ship flat keyword strings or community plugins. NSPredicateEditor gives us the most usable structured-query UI in the category for ~3 days of wiring. This is the demo screenshot.
acceptance_criteria:
- Sidebar "Smart Notes" section populated with built-in queries
- "New Smart Note" sheet uses NSPredicateEditor with templates for the supported fields
- Today smart note correctly surfaces headings with `scheduled <= today AND status != done`
- Smart notes persist across launches as plain-text Smart Notes/{name}.md files
- Predicate is editable round-trip (open existing smart note → re-edit in sheet)

title: Inspector panel (⌘I) for per-heading metadata
description:
SwiftUI inspector pane bound to the heading under the caret. Editable fields: tags, status (todo/doing/done), scheduled, due, custom key-value rows.

On save, writes a `> [!props]` markdown callout block immediately under the heading. For document-root metadata, appends to YAML frontmatter instead.

Why: this is the schema surface that unblocks Smart Notes (predicates query against this), DataDetectors (Today smart note pulls scheduled), and any structured-query feature. Per PunkRecords_Orgmode_Lessons_Translation.md this is Days 1–2 of the 2-week sprint — everything else depends on it.

UX reference: mirrors Reminders.app task detail (title, notes, due, priority, tags, list). Date fields accept natural-language input via NSDataDetector ("next Monday", "+1w", "tomorrow at 3").

Key decision: hard-code status to todo/doing/done (3 states). Custom per-file state sets are an Emacs-tier configurability trap — explicitly cut by the translation guide.
acceptance_criteria:
- ⌘I toggles inspector on the heading under the caret
- Edits round-trip to `> [!props]` callout (or frontmatter at doc root) without breaking markdown rendering
- Date fields parse "next Monday" / "tomorrow" / "+1w" via NSDataDetector
- Caret moving to a different heading updates the inspector live
- Three statuses: todo, doing, done — hard-coded, no user config

title: Wikilinks autocomplete + backlinks panel
description:
Two-part feature on top of the existing MarkdownParser wikilink support.

(1) Autocomplete: typing `[[` in the editor pops a list of matching note titles (fuzzy match on title + aliases); selecting one inserts the wikilink. Keyboard-driven (↑/↓/enter/esc).

(2) Backlinks panel: collapsible section at the bottom of every note showing all notes that link to the current note, with a 1-line context excerpt. Click jumps to the source.

Underlying: SQLite link-graph table updated on file save. Columns: source_path, target_path, target_title, source_line, context_excerpt. Index on target_path. Repository emits VaultChange.linksUpdated so the panel refreshes reactively.
acceptance_criteria:
- Typing `[[` shows autocomplete within 50ms on a 10k-note vault
- Backlinks panel renders within 100ms on note open
- Renaming a note updates link graph and surfaces broken links
- Unresolved [[New Note]] links are visually distinct and click-to-create
notes:
SCOPE NARROWED (PunkRecords_Wysiwyg_research.md ingestion): the in-editor wikilink autocomplete and pill-chip rendering portion of this issue is now owned by PUNK-6du (Editor W4: Wikilink pill chips + tag chips + in-editor autocomplete) as part of the markdown editor epic PUNK-1hy.

What remains in this issue:
- Backlinks panel as a collapsible UI section at the bottom of every note
- Underlying SQLite link-graph table (source_path, target_path, target_title, source_line, context_excerpt) maintained on save
- VaultChange.linksUpdated reactive refresh
- Renaming a note updates link graph; broken links surfaced

The link-graph table is also the data source for the unresolved-link styling and click-to-create flow in PUNK-6du — coordinate so both issues share one schema.
close_reason: Shipped reactive refresh via AppState.vaultChangeTick + count badge in panel header. The autocomplete half of this issue migrated to PUNK-6du (Editor W4) per the editor research; backlinks panel + link-graph (SQLiteSearchIndex.backlinks) was already implemented, this commit adds the missing reactive refresh that prompted the issue.

title: Global quick-capture hotkey
description:
System-wide hotkey opens a floating composer; on submit, a new .md note is written to the vault with auto-frontmatter (id, created, source: quick-capture). Goal: <300ms from hotkey to text cursor.

Why: capture friction kills PKMs. If writing a thought down costs more than one keystroke, the thought dies. This is the cheapest, highest-leverage P0 we can ship.

Implementation notes: NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents for the hotkey; floating SwiftUI window (NSPanel + .floatingPanel style); on submit, write via the existing repository actor; synchronously add to FTS5 index so the note is searchable immediately. Hotkey is configurable in Settings; default ⌃⌥⌘N.
acceptance_criteria:
- Hotkey from any app opens composer in <300ms
- Submit writes file + indexes synchronously
- Esc dismisses without saving
- Hotkey is rebindable; conflicts surfaced to user
notes:
Per PunkRecords_Orgmode_Lessons_Translation.md, this issue is the hotkey + floating composer leg of the macOS-native "capture trinity":
- PUNK-5pr (this) — global hotkey + status item + floating composer
- PUNK-37e — capture templates (filterable picker on this composer)
- PUNK-5xp — Share Sheet extension from Safari/etc.
- PUNK-tjl — Services menu + App Intents (Shortcuts)

Scope to add here: the menu-bar NSStatusItem that opens the same composer. Hotkey and status-item click should both land on the same floating panel.

title: Web-summary note schema + writer + paste-URL trigger
description:
Storage + UX entry point for the URL→summary feature.

Schema (Web/{YYYY-MM-DD}-{slug}.md): YAML frontmatter with title, source_url, source_domain, author, published, fetched (ISO8601), fetcher (tier name), summary_model, summary_prompt_version, word_count, reading_time_min, tags, type: web-summary, status, links (wikilinks).

Consider whether implementation-detail fields (fetcher, summary_model, summary_prompt_version) should live under a _punkrecords: namespace to keep top-level frontmatter clean for users. Recommend doing so.

UX triggers:
- Detect URL in chat composer; show inline "Summarize this URL" affordance
- Command palette / menu: "Summarize URL from clipboard"
- Future: share extension and quick-capture URL paste path

Progress UI: live status (fetching → extracting → summarizing → writing) with a Cancel button.
acceptance_criteria:
- Paste URL into chat → inline button appears → click → progress UI → note created in Web/ within 10s for a typical article
- Frontmatter validates against schema (snapshot test)
- _punkrecords: namespace keeps user-visible frontmatter clean

title: Structured summary prompt + citation rendering
description:
Prompt + post-processor that takes WebFetchTool output and produces the 4-section markdown body with inline citations.

Output sections:
1. TL;DR (1–3 sentences)
2. Key points (5–9 bullets, each with inline [¹] citation)
3. Notable quotes (2–4 verbatim excerpts ≤30 words)
4. Why this matters / open questions (optional)

Citation strategy: for each bullet/quote, the LLM emits a JSON tuple { citation_index, supporting_text, nearest_heading_anchor }. Post-processor converts to #:~:text=<url-encoded supporting_text>; if supporting_text is non-unique on the page, prepend prefix-/-suffix per W3C scroll-to-text-fragment spec.

Must be deterministic enough that re-summarizing the same URL with the same model + prompt_version produces identical citations (modulo LLM nondeterminism). Store prompt_version in frontmatter.

Provider-agnostic: works with Anthropic, OpenAI, and Apple Foundation Models. Use structured outputs / tool-call schema where available.
acceptance_criteria:
- Given a long-form article, output passes structural validation: 4 sections, every bullet has a citation, every citation resolves to a unique passage on the page
- Snapshot tests across 5 reference articles pin the structural output (eval-tagged)
- Re-running with same prompt_version + model produces byte-equal Sources section in ≥80% of trials

title: WebFetchTool: three-tier extraction ladder (SwiftSoup → WKWebView → Jina)
description:
Build the fetcher that turns a URL into clean, structured markdown for downstream summarization.

Tier 1 (default, offline, private): URLSession + SwiftSoup + Readability-style scoring ported to Swift (~400 lines). Fast, no JS rendering.
Tier 2 (fallback for JS-rendered or sparse pages): invisible WKWebView; inject @mozilla/readability after load; evaluate parse function. Detect trigger via isProbablyReaderable() returning false OR Tier 1 content < ~250 chars.
Tier 3 (escape hatch, opt-in only): Jina Reader API (r.jina.ai/<URL>) — explicit user consent dialog noting URL leaves the device.

Output shape: { title, byline, contentMarkdown, headings: [(level, text, anchor_id)], extractedAt, tier, sourceURL, canonicalURL }. The headings array drives citation fallback. Cache HTML in Web/_cache/{slug}.html for offline anchor rendering.

Shares ground with PUNK-e5u (which scoped a similar tool for web_search fallback) — coordinate so we have one WebFetchTool, not two.
acceptance_criteria:
- Given 20 sample URLs (mix of blogs, docs sites, SPAs, news), Tier 1 succeeds on >70% within 1s
- Tier 2 triggers automatically when Tier 1 underperforms; succeeds on JS-rendered pages
- Tier 3 requires per-URL user consent; consent persisted per-domain
- Output preserves heading structure precisely (each h1–h3 with anchor id or generated slug)

title: Epic: URL → cliffs-notes web summary feature
description:
Wedge feature: user pastes a URL → agent fetches the page → produces a structured summary (TL;DR + key points + quotes) → stored as a markdown note in the vault with per-bullet citations that link back to the source paragraph via #:~:text= fragments.

Why: differentiator versus Bear/Apple Notes/Obsidian; no existing tool combines (a) one-paste ingest, (b) structured summary, (c) per-bullet jump-to-source, (d) plain-markdown storage. Recall.it is closest but cloud-only.

Scope tracked via sub-issues: fetcher ladder, summary prompt, citation rendering, storage schema/UI, failure modes. See PunkRecords_Features_Research.md §3.
acceptance_criteria:
- Paste a URL anywhere it makes sense (chat composer, quick-capture, command palette) and get a markdown note created in Web/{YYYY-MM-DD}-{slug}.md within 10s for a typical article
- Note has TL;DR + 5–9 key points + 2–4 quotes + Sources section
- Each bullet has an inline numbered citation [¹] resolving to a #:~:text= URL that, when opened in Safari, scrolls to the cited paragraph
- Works offline-first for static pages; falls back to WKWebView for JS-heavy pages; offers Jina Reader as a final tier with explicit user consent

title: Emacs editing commands (undo, save, case, transpose)
description: Round out Emacs mode with common editing chords: C-/ (and C-_) undo, C-x C-s save buffer, M-c/M-u/M-l capitalize/upcase/downcase word, M-t transpose words (C-t transpose chars is native), C-o open-line, C-x u undo. Implements the C-x prefix handling needed for C-x C-s / C-x u. Scope is in-buffer editing only — no file-manager or M-x command palette.
acceptance_criteria: With Emacs mode on: C-/ undoes, C-x C-s saves, M-c/M-u/M-l transform the next word's case, M-t transposes words, C-x prefix sequences dispatch correctly.
close_reason: Editing commands done. Core: pure EmacsEdit (caseEdit for M-c/M-u/M-l, transposeWords for M-t; 9 tests). Coordinator: C-/ and C-_ undo, C-x prefix (handleEmacsChord state machine) with C-x C-s save + C-x u undo, M-c/M-u/M-l case, M-t transpose. PillTextView now dispatches raw chords so prefixes resolve in the coordinator. performEmacsCommand switch is now exhaustive over EmacsCommand. Tests: Core 136 green; build + lint clean.

title: Preview: theme alignment with editor (Dracula + dark mode)
description: The preview uses MarkdownUI's stock .gitHub theme, which clashes with the editor's Dracula theme and is not dark-mode adaptive. Build a MarkdownUI Theme mirroring EditorTheme (background, foreground, heading colors, code background, link color) so editor and preview look consistent, honoring the active editor theme and system appearance.
acceptance_criteria: Preview background/text/heading/code/link colors match the active editor theme; readable in both light and dark mode.
close_reason: Added MarkdownUI.Theme.punk(EditorTheme) deriving preview colors from the editor theme (background, text, headings via headingColors, inline code fg/bg, links, blockquote). MarkdownPreviewView now uses .punk(theme) + theme.background instead of .gitHub. UI-only styling — no unit test; needs visual verification in light/dark.

title: Code-fence highlighting for Bash and JSON
description: Extend fenced-code highlighting to bash and json (common in a dev knowledge base; the user's test note has both). Both ship clean SwiftPM support with self-contained highlight queries: tree-sitter-bash v0.23.3, tree-sitter-json v0.24.3 (last json tag before SPM support was dropped).
close_reason: Added tree-sitter-bash 0.23.3 (bash/sh/shell/zsh) and tree-sitter-json 0.24.3 (json/jsonc) — both clean SPM deps with self-contained highlight queries. Bundles verified embedded in the .app. Tests: Infra highlighter suite 20 green.

title: Code-fence highlighting for TypeScript, Rust, C, C++ (+ aliases)
description: Fenced-code syntax highlighting only covers swift/python/javascript. The user's test note uses typescript, rust, cpp fences which fall through to no grammar. Add tree-sitter grammars for TypeScript (inherits JS queries), Rust, C, and C++ (inherits C queries), plus common fence aliases (ts, rs, cpp/c++/cc, js/jsx). TS/C++ need parent+child highlight-query concatenation since their highlights.scm only carry derived rules.
close_reason: Added tree-sitter grammars for TypeScript (+ts/mts/cts), Rust (+rs), C (+h), C++ (+c++/cc/cxx/hpp/...), plus jsx/mjs JS aliases. TS and C++ highlights.scm only carry derived rules, so makeInheritedCodeConfiguration concatenates parent+child highlight queries (JS→TS, C→C++) and compiles them against the child grammar, with graceful fallback to child-only. Grammar bundles verified embedded in the .app with queries. Tests: Infra 111 green (incl. new resolution + merge tests).

title: User-customizable editor themes (theme picker + persistence)
description: The editor now ships a built-in EditorTheme with a .dracula preset (and a system .default), wired through TreeSitterMarkdownHighlighter, HybridUXDecorator, and WikilinkDecorator. This issue is the full custom-theme system on top of that foundation: (1) a Settings/preferences theme picker listing built-in presets (Dracula, system default, + room for Solarized/Nord/etc.); (2) persistence of the chosen theme (per-vault or app-wide via RecentVaultsStore/UserDefaults); (3) optionally, user-defined themes — editable palette (background, foreground, heading/strong/emphasis/code/link/marker colors) saved to disk; (4) apply live without reopening the vault. EditorTheme already bundles every color the editor uses, so presets are cheap to add. Currently the editor is hardcoded to .dracula in EditorTextViewRepresentable.

title: Editor accessibility + IME verification pass
description: Deferred from W6 (PUNK-gf2). Manual audit: (1) VoiceOver navigation over headings, links, and pill-chip wikilinks; add accessibilityLabel to any attachment-based chips if math/attachments adopt NSTextAttachment. Note: current wikilink/tag pills are attribute-based (not attachments), so VoiceOver reads the underlying source text already. (2) IME composition test with Japanese (kotoeri), Simplified Chinese (pinyin), Korean (2-Set) inside paragraphs with adjacent markdown markers. A guard already skips decoration while hasMarkedText() is true; verify it holds across the three IMEs.

title: Inline + block math rendering ($x^2$, $$...$$)
description: Deferred from W6 (PUNK-gf2). Render inline `$x^2$` and block `$$...$$` LaTeX as NSTextAttachment instances (SwiftMath renders to NSImage/NSView — better fit than LaTeXSwiftUI for TextKit attachments). Source stays editable; preview re-renders on edit. The slash palette already inserts a $$ math block skeleton (the 'Math Block' command). Needs a new SPM dep.

title: Click-to-create for unresolved wikilinks
description: Deferred from W4 (PUNK-6du). A click on an unresolved [[Nonexistent]] pill prompts to create the note (or creates it directly) and opens it. Builds on the existing PillTextView click hit-testing and the vault create-note flow (AppState.createNewNote / repository).
close_reason: Clicking an unresolved [[wikilink]] now prompts (NSAlert) to create a note titled after the link; confirming creates it (H1 + frontmatter title match the link so it resolves afterward) and opens it. Added WikilinkDecorator.ClickAction (.open/.create) + clickAction(at:in:) — unit-tested. PillTextView routes clicks through it; AppState.createNote(titled:) builds on the tested repository.save + FilenameHelpers.uniqueNotePath primitives. 19 wikilink tests pass.

title: Tag autocomplete + click-to-filter
description: Deferred from W4 (PUNK-6du). (1) Autocomplete on `#` once the third char is typed, backed by a tags table from the background indexer. (2) Clicking a #tag pill filters notes by tag (or opens a tag view — tag view itself is a separate issue). Tag pills + hash-derived colors already shipped in W4.
close_reason: Tag autocomplete (#-trigger popover reusing the wikilink completion machinery, vault tags as source) + click-to-filter (#tag pill click sets sidebar query to tag:<name>, SidebarFilter gained tag: prefix support). Pure logic in Core TagAutocomplete + SidebarFilter, tag hit-testing in WikilinkDecorator. Tested: Core 14, Infra WikilinkDecorator 23, Integration SidebarFilter 14.

title: Wikilink autocomplete popover (`[[` trigger)
description: Deferred from W4 (PUNK-6du) visual core. Typing `[[` opens a fuzzy-match popover anchored to the caret, sourced from vault titles (reuse QuickOpenMatcher). Up/Down/Enter selects and inserts `[[Title]]`; Esc dismisses; results update as the user types after `[[`. Anchor the NSPopover/overlay to the caret rect via NSLayoutManager boundingRect(forGlyphRange:in:).
close_reason: Typing [[ opens a caret-anchored completion popover (non-activating NSPanel hosting a SwiftUI list) with fuzzy matches over vault titles (QuickOpenMatcher). ↑/↓ navigate, Enter/Tab insert [[Title]] and place the caret past the closing brackets, Esc dismisses, clicks select. Live-updates as you type after [[. WikilinkAutocomplete (Core) does the pure [[-session detection (10 tests) — handles spaces in titles, stops at ]/newline/|, picks the most-recent opener. PillTextView routes keys via doCommand(by:) while visible. 77 Core tests pass; build clean. NOTE: popover positioning/interaction polish needs a visual check in-app.

title: Code-fence content syntax highlighting via tree-sitter language injection
description:
Acceptance carved out of W3 (PUNK-zp2) so the decorator pass could ship cleanly. Wires per-language tree-sitter grammars (swift, python, javascript at minimum) through Neon's languageProvider so fenced code blocks get colored content based on the info-string language tag. Token-name mapping per language is the main effort.

Approach: add SPM deps for tree-sitter-swift (alex-pinkus), tree-sitter-python, tree-sitter-javascript. Extend TreeSitterMarkdownHighlighter.makeLanguageProvider to map 'swift'/'python'/'javascript'/'js' to their LanguageConfigurations using the existing locateQueriesDirectory fallback. Expand the attribute provider to recognize common capture names (keyword, string, comment, function, type, variable, operator, number).

Beware: tree-sitter/swift-tree-sitter vs ChimeHQ/SwiftTreeSitter identity conflict (the python/javascript grammars depend on tree-sitter/; Neon depends on ChimeHQ/). SPM likely resolves them as the same identity with a warning — verify on first SPM resolve.
close_reason: Fenced code blocks now get syntax-colored content via tree-sitter language injection for swift, python, and javascript (js/py aliases too). Added the three grammar SPM deps; pinned python→0.23.6 and javascript→0.23.1 (tagged releases list src/scanner.c explicitly — master branches use a CWD-relative fileExists that drops the external scanner under SPM and fails to link), and swift→0.7.2-with-generated-files (ships the generated parser.c). languageProvider maps fence languages to LanguageConfigurations via the existing bundle-discovery fallback. Theme gained a codeColors palette keyed by capture leading-component (keyword/string/comment/function/type/number/…), wired to the Dracula palette. 17 highlighter tests (incl. grammar loading + color mapping); 104 Infra tests pass.

title: Welcome window + first-run onboarding polish
description:
Polish the first-run experience and the welcome window mentioned in CLAUDE.md.

Why: surfaced during backlog audit — the welcome window exists (per CLAUDE.md "Welcome window (CMD+0) — vault selection/creation via RecentVaultsStore") but the first-run flow is not tracked as a polish issue. New users (incl. wife) deserve a clear path from app-launch to first-typed-note.

Scope:
- Vault picker: "Open existing" / "Create new" / Recent vaults list
- Create-new flow: location picker, optional template ("Empty" / "Sample with starter notes")
- API key prompt with a clearly visible "Skip — use Apple Intelligence" option (so users without API keys can immediately use Foundation Models)
- Quick-tour card on first vault open: keyboard shortcuts, capture hotkey, chat panel, sparse-tree search
- "Show again" toggle for the tour card
- Wired to RecentVaultsStore (already exists)
acceptance_criteria:
- First launch shows the welcome window
- Creating a new vault produces a working vault with optional sample notes
- A user without API keys can open a vault and type a note without seeing any error or modal
- Keyboard shortcut tour card is dismissible and remembers the dismissal

title: Export to HTML / PDF / shareable
description:
Export a note (or selection) to HTML or PDF for sharing outside the vault.

Why: surfaced during backlog audit — Marked-2-style "preview then share" is a baseline expectation. The vault is plain markdown, but recipients usually want a styled artifact. Reuses swift-markdown (already a dep of the editor epic) for canonical rendering.

Scope:
- Render to HTML via swift-markdown's HTMLFormatter (or a custom walker for our extensions like callouts, wikilink chips)
- PDF: render the HTML into a WKWebView, print to PDF via NSPrintOperation, or use Apple's newer PDFKit document generation
- Share Sheet integration: ⌘⇧S opens an export dialog → format → save or share via NSSharingService
- Bundle assets: embedded images via base64 in HTML (single-file export) or accompanying images folder

Deferred: presentation-mode export (slideshow), epub export, Pages/Word interop.
acceptance_criteria:
- File menu: Export → HTML, Export → PDF
- HTML export is a single self-contained file with embedded images
- PDF export matches the visible editor styling (or a print stylesheet)
- Wikilinks render as visible text + tooltip showing the target note name (not active links in exported docs)
- Frontmatter optionally included or stripped via toggle
close_reason: Shipped HTML export with self-contained styling. cmark-gfm-backed renderer in Core (MarkdownHTMLRenderer), File menu 'Export as HTML…' with Cmd+Shift+E, VaultWindow NSSavePanel handler. 10 unit tests for the renderer. PDF export deferred to a follow-up — the renderer's HTML is print-stylesheet ready so PDF can layer on via WKWebView+printToPDF when needed.

title: Autosave + crash recovery for edited notes
description:
Debounced autosave on idle (1–2s after last edit) + crash recovery via sidecar file.

Why: surfaced during backlog audit — the editor exists but there is no tracked autosave / recovery story. A crash mid-edit loses everything since the last manual save. Standard PKM expectation.

Implementation outline:
- Debounce: 1.5s of no edits → write to disk. Plus periodic 30s safety save while typing.
- Recovery: write to `.{filename}.swp` or `{vault}/.punkrecords/recovery/{note-id}.md` first, then atomic rename to target. On launch, scan recovery dir; if any recovery files newer than their target, surface "Recover unsaved changes?" UI per file.
- Dirty state already exists in DocumentEditorViewModel; this issue formalizes the persistence layer.
- Conflict with iCloud Drive sync (PUNK-t4g): atomic write avoids most issues, but document the interaction.
acceptance_criteria:
- Editing a note + force-quitting the app leaves a recovery sidecar
- Relaunch surfaces a recovery dialog listing recoverable notes
- Accepting recovery restores the in-memory unsaved state
- Atomic write semantics — never leave a partial file on crash mid-save

title: Image storage convention for vault notes
description:
Define and implement the on-disk convention for images dropped/pasted into a note.

Proposed convention: `{vault}/attachments/{note-slug}/{timestamp-uuid}.{ext}` with the markdown line emitting `![alt](attachments/{note-slug}/{filename})` using vault-relative paths. Alternative considered: per-note `_attachments/` sibling folder (cleaner co-location, more clutter). Pick one and document it.

Why: surfaced during backlog audit — Editor W6 includes math attachments, the chat composer (PUNK-tpy) handles user-attached images, and quick-capture (PUNK-5pr) will probably want screenshot capture. There is no single source of truth for "where do image bytes live in this vault." Decide once.

Scope: convention + writer + collision handling (same filename → uuid suffix) + .gitignore template hint (don't ignore attachments by default).

This is distinct from PUNK-tpy (chat attachments are read-only references to external files; this issue covers content the user creates inside their notes).
acceptance_criteria:
- A document describing the convention exists in the repo (CLAUDE.md addition or docs/conventions.md)
- Drag/paste of an image into a note results in a file at the documented path and a markdown reference using the documented relative path syntax
- Identical filenames in the same note do not overwrite (uuid suffix or sequential disambiguation)
- Existing notes with image links continue to render correctly
close_reason: Shipped VaultPaths in Core/Utilities defining the canonical attachments/{note-stem}/{filename} convention with collision handling, sanitization, and markdown reference formatting. 10 unit tests. CLAUDE.md updated with the contract so future paste/drop handlers + screenshot capture + agentic writes all share a single source of truth.

title: Settings / preferences UI
description:
A central Settings window covering: API key entry per provider (Anthropic, OpenAI) via existing KeychainService; default LLM provider + model (Apple Foundation Models / Claude / GPT); global quick-capture hotkey configuration; default vault path; editor font + theme selection; autosave interval; gitleaks scan toggle.

Why: surfaced during backlog audit — multiple in-flight features (quick-capture hotkey, multi-provider picker, KeychainService) need user-facing config and there is no settings surface tracked. Without this, users edit defaults via terminal or app launch flags.

SwiftUI macOS Settings scene: `Settings { TabView { … } }`. Keychain integration already exists; this issue is the UI on top.
acceptance_criteria:
- ⌘, opens a tabbed Settings window
- General / Providers / Editor / Keyboard tabs at minimum
- API keys stored via KeychainService; never written to disk in plain text
- Provider selection persists across launches
- Hotkey rebinding has a conflict detector against system shortcuts

title: Editor W6: Math attachments + slash commands + AX/IME/perf audit
description:
Polish + the v1 cuts. Acceptable to defer items into v1.1 as they fall out of scope.

Deliverables:
- Inline math `$x^2$` and block math `$$...$$` rendered via SwiftMath or LaTeXSwiftUI as NSTextAttachment instances. Source remains editable; preview re-renders on edit.
- Slash command palette skeleton: `/` at line start (or after whitespace) opens a small popover with a built-in command list (heading, list, code block, table skeleton, callout, math, wikilink, attachment). Extensible structure but no plugin API in v1.
- Accessibility audit: VoiceOver pass on headings, links, attachments; rotor entries; ensure pill-chip attachments expose accessibilityLabel.
- IME testing: manual pass with Japanese (kotoeri), Simplified Chinese (pinyin), Korean (Hangul 2-Set). Common failure point on custom editors — Apple's NSTextInputClient on NSTextView handles this, but verify with composed text spanning markdown markers.
- Performance test: 10MB synthetic markdown file (Faker-generated); 50k-note vault for autocomplete pressure.

Deferred to v1.1 (explicitly not in this week):
- Mermaid / PlantUML / Excalidraw block rendering (ship as plain fenced code blocks for v1)
- Table editor with paste-from-Excel detection
- Properties panel UI on top of frontmatter (the Inspector PUNK-4bz writes callouts; a frontmatter form is separate)
- Block references `[[Note^block-id]]`
- Custom themes
acceptance_criteria:
- Inline + block math renders to readable formula glyphs without breaking source editability
- `/` opens slash command popover with at least 8 built-in commands
- VoiceOver navigates headings, links, and pill-chip wikilinks correctly
- Japanese/Chinese/Korean IME composition works inside a paragraph with adjacent markdown markers
- Editing a 10MB file remains responsive (incremental highlight p99 < 50ms; scroll p99 < 16ms)
- Autocomplete on a 50k-note vault < 50ms p99
close_reason: Delivered the testable core: (1) Slash command palette — '/' at line-start/after-whitespace opens a native NSMenu of 13 built-in commands (headings, lists, task, quote, code, table, divider, wikilink, math, callout); selection replaces the /query and places the caret. SlashCommandLibrary (Core) with trigger detection + fuzzy filter, 14 tests. (2) Performance: decoration was O(doc) per keystroke (175s on 10MB!); now limited to the visible range + buffer via EditorDecorationRange — 10MB decorates in ~3.5ms, well under the 50ms frame budget. Scroll re-decorates the newly visible region. 3 perf tests. (3) IME safety: decoration skips while hasMarkedText() so composition isn't disrupted. Deferred to follow-ups: math attachment rendering (PUNK-1oo), full VoiceOver+IME verification pass (PUNK-cmd).

title: Quick Look generator for vault notes
description:
QuickLookThumbnailing extension renders a vault note's frontmatter + callouts (especially `[!props]`) as a styled preview. Triggered by spacebar in Finder, attachments in Mail, AirDrop, Spotlight results.

Why: per PunkRecords_Orgmode_Lessons_Translation.md, Quick Look is the most underused integration in Mac apps. Letting other apps see vault data without opening PunkRecords is the macOS-native answer to "the format is the API."

Keep the renderer simple: title, frontmatter property table, first paragraph, wikilinks as styled chips (non-clickable in QL view). Match the in-app styling for `[!props]` and `[!note]` callouts so previews look continuous with the app.
acceptance_criteria:
- Spacebar on a vault .md file in Finder shows a styled preview
- Preview renders title, top-level frontmatter properties, and the first paragraph
- Wikilinks visible as styled chips (non-interactive in QL)
- `[!props]` and `[!note]` callouts render with the same look as the main app
- Works on .md files outside the vault too (graceful degradation: no property table, just rendered markdown)

title: Services menu + App Intents (Shortcuts) for system-wide capture/automation
description:
Two complementary system integrations completing the capture trinity:

1. Services menu (NSApp.servicesProvider) — select text in any app → Services → "New PunkRecords note from selection."
2. App Intents (Shortcuts.app) — expose Capture Note, Summarize URL, Search Vault as Shortcut actions. Users build their own automation.

Why: per PunkRecords_Orgmode_Lessons_Translation.md, the macOS-native capture trinity is status item + global hotkey (PUNK-5pr) + Share Sheet (PUNK-5xp) + Services + Shortcuts. This issue covers the Services + Shortcuts legs; PUNK-5pr and PUNK-5xp cover the other two.

Acceptance leans on Shortcuts.app and the `shortcuts` CLI being available for testing the actions deterministically.
acceptance_criteria:
- Services menu shows "New PunkRecords note from selection" globally (Safari, Mail, Messages, TextEdit, etc.)
- Shortcuts.app surfaces at least three PunkRecords actions: Capture Note, Summarize URL, Search Vault
- Actions are testable via `shortcuts run <name>` from the CLI
- Capture Note action accepts text + optional template name + optional tags

title: DataDetectors-powered date recognition in notes
description:
NSDataDetector runs over note content; recognized dates are underlined on hover with a contextual popover offering: "Schedule" (writes the scheduled property on the nearest heading via the Inspector schema), "Add to Calendar" (EventKit), "Surface in Today smart note."

Inspector date fields accept the same natural-language input via NSDataDetector.

Why: per PunkRecords_Orgmode_Lessons_Translation.md Day 10. macOS has had DataDetectors since 10.5; the framework recognizing dates/addresses/phone numbers in arbitrary text has been hardened for 17 years. The Today smart note picks up scheduled headings via predicate — no new agenda view to build.
acceptance_criteria:
- Dates underlined on hover in the note renderer
- Popover offers: Schedule / Add to Calendar / Surface in Today
- Inspector date fields parse "next Monday" / "tomorrow" / "+2w"
- Today smart note correctly lists headings whose scheduled <= today AND status != done
- "Add to Calendar" creates an EKEvent and writes its identifier back into the heading's property block

title: MCP server exposing vault tools (search/read/create/list/summarize_url)
description:
Lightweight stdio MCP server that exposes the existing AgentTools (VaultSearchTool, ReadDocumentTool, CreateNoteTool, ListDocumentsTool) plus summarize_url to any MCP-aware client (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Claude Code, etc.).

Why: future-proofs the agent surface. Once it exists, you (the developer) can dogfood new workflows from Claude Code without writing dedicated UI for each. Also a credible "AI-native" marker for users who already live in Claude Desktop.

Research ranked this P2; bumping to P1 specifically because (a) it accelerates your own dogfooding velocity, (b) the lift is small given the AgentTool protocol already exists, (c) Swift MCP SDK should be reasonably mature by mid-2026. If the Swift SDK is still rough, ship a Node sidecar.

Implementation: thin wrapper around the existing tools. Read-only by default; opt-in writes (create_note) per session via a config flag. Vault path is the working directory; auth via filesystem permissions.
acceptance_criteria:
- `punkrecords-mcp` binary speaks MCP over stdio
- Claude Desktop can be configured to use it; tools appear in the tool list
- Read tools work out of the box; create_note requires --writable flag
- One-page README documents Claude Desktop and Claude Code setup

title: Daily briefing: scheduled vault summary
description:
Once-per-day generated note summarizing what is interesting / unfinished / worth re-reading in the vault.

Prompt input: notes created or modified in the last 7 days + any notes with open checkboxes / "TODO" / "open thread" markers. Output: 5-bullet brief written to Briefings/{YYYY-MM-DD}.md.

Why: NotebookLM's killer demo is "ask the agent what is interesting in my corpus" — no local-first PKM ships this. Cheap and high-perceived-value if it works.

Default provider: Apple Foundation Models (private, free, no network). Upgrade path: explicit "regenerate with Claude/GPT" button.

Scheduling: simple launch-time check (last_briefing_at < 24h ago → generate). Background scheduling via BGAppRefreshTask is a follow-up — not P1.

Risk: speculative — no proof users want this. Hold this issue until at least one of {Mark, wife} explicitly asks for it OR until P0 ships and we have time to experiment. Keep open in backlog.
acceptance_criteria:
- Manual "Generate today's briefing" command produces a Briefings/{date}.md within 15s using Apple Foundation Models
- Auto-generation on app launch when last briefing > 24h old
- Briefing has 5 bullets, each linked to a source note via [[wikilink]]
- User can regenerate with a different provider in one click

title: Web Clipper share extension (forwards URL to summarize_url)
description:
macOS Share Extension that, when invoked from Safari/Chrome/Firefox, sends the current URL to the running PunkRecords main app, which then triggers the same summarize_url flow as the in-app paste path.

Why: ~70% of "web summary" use is "I am reading this — save it," not "I have a URL on my clipboard." A one-click clipper is the natural surface.

Implementation: ShareExtension target (SwiftUI). IPC: main app listens on a local Unix socket (or XPC mach service); extension posts {url, optional selected_text} to it; if the app isn't running, extension launches it via LSOpenURL. If the user selected text before sharing, include it as a hint to bias the summary toward that section.

Bonus: Safari Reader-API integration — if the page is in Reader Mode, prefer Safari's extracted content over our own fetcher (skip Tier 1).
acceptance_criteria:
- Share Extension appears in macOS share sheet from Safari/Chrome/Firefox
- Clicking it produces a note in the vault (same shape as in-app paste path)
- Selected text is recorded as a hint and included in the summary prompt
- Works when main app is closed (cold-launch path)

title: iCloud Drive vault location support
description:
Allow the vault folder to live in ~/Library/Mobile Documents/iCloud~com~punkrecords~vault/ (or any iCloud-backed path) so two devices/users share notes without a custom sync server.

Why: rolling our own E2E sync is months of work; iCloud Drive is free and battle-tested. Section 4 of the research flags subscription fatigue and lock-in as the dominant user complaints; "your vault is a folder in iCloud" is the honest answer.

Work involved:
- Add a known iCloud container to the app entitlements + Info.plist
- Vault picker UI: detect iCloud paths and label them as such
- File watcher (DispatchSource.makeFileSystemObjectSource): handle iCloud download-on-demand (files may appear as placeholders)
- Conflict handling: Apple's "filename (Conflict 1).md" copies surface in a dedicated "Conflicts" view; do NOT auto-merge — last-writer-wins per file with conflicts visible
- Re-index FTS5 on file change
acceptance_criteria:
- Welcome window offers "New Vault in iCloud" alongside local-folder option
- Two Macs signed into the same iCloud see changes propagate within ~30s
- Conflict files appear in a dedicated UI; user can pick a winner
- File watcher handles placeholder-state files without crashing
